journal_entry.rationale: Inventory loss from natural disaster is recognized as an expense (loss). The debit tuple (0,0,1,0,0,0) indicates one expense increase line. The credit tuple (0,0,0,1,0,0) indicates one asset decrease line. Since inventory was destroyed, we debit Loss on Inventory (expense account) and credit Inventory (asset decrease). No tax applies to inventory write-offs under Canadian tax rules. Total debits ($2,000) = total credits ($2,000).

ambiguities.resolution: 'loss' terminology implies uninsured — insured events are described as claims or insurance receivables, not losses

ambiguities.aspect: whether inventory was recorded at cost or other value
ambiguities.resolution: $2000 stated as 'loss' implies this is the carrying amount to be written off — inventory is recorded at cost under standard convention

output.reason: Inventory destroyed = asset decrease. The inventory (an asset) is written off due to the typhoon damage, so inventory account is credited.

credit_classifier.reason: Inventory destroyed = asset decrease. The inventory (an asset) is written off due to the typhoon damage, so inventory account is credited.

output.reason: Inventory destroyed by typhoon represents a casualty loss. The debit is to an expense account (loss from casualty/disaster) which is an expense increase. The inventory asset will be credited (decreased) on the credit side.
